quote: No. This is Debbie. It's wife.
stewart_read: Debbie answering for Mike is the structural origin of the call's biggest unresolved problem. The rep never asked whether Debbie or Mike is on the title, never confirmed who the decision-maker is, and never attempted to include Mike in any next step until Debbie herself raised him at 02:55. The verify.title_co_owners item was never completed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have intended to surface the title question later in the verify flow. Rejected — the call ended without the question ever being asked, and the button-up made no attempt to include Mike.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Bucky that the spouse-coordination protocol starts at the first signal, not at button-up. When Debbie said 'It's wife,' the correct move was to note Mike's name and flag him as a required participant before the call ended. Manager should walk through protocols.spouse_decision with Bucky using this exact exchange.

quote: I kinda wanted to get a sense of what it costs because other people are asking about it. That I do own that property, but I wasn't particularly interested.
stewart_read: Debbie's referral-network signal — 'other people are asking about it' — is not covered by a current schema section (TBD gap; closest to analysis_directives.trust_building_micro_trades). This is not a personal solar motivation (verify.interest_in_solar does not apply here). However, it is a meaningful qualifier: Debbie owns the property, is embedded in a social network where solar is being discussed, and is doing research on behalf of others. A rep who recognized this could have reframed the conversation: 'So you're the one people come to with questions — let's make sure you have the real numbers, not just an estimator.' That reframe creates a reason for Debbie to engage that fits her actual posture.
alternative_hypothesis: The referral signal could be dismissed as deflection — Debbie positioning herself as a non-buyer to exit the call. Rejected: she confirmed property ownership in the same breath, which is a genuine qualifier signal.
coaching_implication: Bucky walked past the most actionable piece of information on the call. The manager should ask: 'What would you have done differently if you'd heard her say she owns the property and people are asking her about solar?' The goal is to get Bucky to see the referral-network framing as a curiosity-creation entry point, not a disqualifier.

quote: Yeah. And then you know about the bill swap too?
stewart_read: Bucky's bill-swap pitch is delivered before any verify items are completed — no address, no title confirmation, no bill, no roof age, nothing. The pitch is also feature-forward ('we replace your power bill with a solar bill') rather than curiosity-creating. The schema is explicit: setters should make customers curious to see their specific math, not explain the product. Bucky is selling solar to someone who said she wasn't interested, without any personalized anchor to make the pitch relevant.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been using the bill-swap explanation to create enough interest to continue the call before running verify. Rejected — the explanation is generic and does not reference anything Debbie said, so it cannot function as a curiosity hook.
coaching_implication: Manager should contrast this moment with the schema's curiosity_over_features principle. The coaching question for Bucky: 'What did you know about Debbie's bill, her roof, or her home before you pitched the bill-swap program?' The answer is nothing — and that's the gap.

quote: because the the state did try to, extend the solar credit. Okay. And, I don't think that worked out real well, but I think the feds are the ones that cut off the solar credit for residential.
stewart_read: Debbie is operating on incomplete or incorrect information about the federal tax credit. The schema's tax_credit_disclosure note directly applies: Ion can no longer guarantee the tax credit on purchased systems, but installation prior to year-end may still allow the customer to claim it, and purchase options without the credit exist. Bucky's response ('Okay. So the the concern there. Yeah. I mean, we can...') trails off without correcting the misinformation or using it as a curiosity-creation bridge. This was a live opening: 'Actually, there's still a window — that's exactly what our specialist would walk you through. Want to get on the calendar before that window closes?'
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky may have been avoiding a technical topic outside setter scope. Partially valid — but the schema's tax_credit_disclosure is setter-level content, and the urgency framing ('window before year-end') is a legitimate curiosity hook, not a closer-tier explanation.
coaching_implication: Manager should pull up qualifiers.notes.tax_credit_disclosure and show Bucky the exact language. The coaching point: you don't need to explain the tax credit — you need to use the customer's uncertainty about it as a reason to get them in front of the specialist. The urgency framing was available and unused.

quote: I I just like I said, I like I don't wanna put you with a bunch of work. I just wanted to look at what the cost would be for a small home like that.
stewart_read: Debbie's 'I don't wanna put you with a bunch of work' is a guilt-as-objection pattern (analysis_directives.guilt_as_objection_detection) — a polite soft refusal framed as consideration for the rep. Bucky's response ('it wouldn't be too much work, it's all complimentary') addresses the surface framing but misses the underlying signal: Debbie is looking for permission to exit. The objection_inversion move was available: flip 'I don't want to waste your time' into 'the only thing that would waste both our time is you not knowing what your specific numbers look like.' Instead, Bucky reassured her and moved on.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky's reassurance could be read as a deliberate low-pressure move to keep Debbie on the line. Rejected — the reassurance did not create any new curiosity or forward motion; Debbie's next substantive line is her exit at 02:55.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to introduce the guilt-as-objection reframe. The coaching question: 'When a customer says they don't want to waste your time, what are they actually telling you?' The answer is: they're looking for a reason to stay, framed as a reason to leave. The flip gives them the reason to stay.

quote: We have teams everywhere. So, like, even where you're at, but me personally, I'm in Utah.
stewart_read: Bucky volunteered his Utah location unprompted in response to Debbie's question about where the company is based. The disclosure immediately prompted Debbie to ask 'So the company's in Utah?' — a legitimacy question the rep then had to walk back. The intros.standard section establishes that legitimacy must be anchored early; this moment did the opposite by introducing geographic distance as a concern. The correct move was a simple, confident answer: 'We're a national company — we have a team in your area.' No personal location needed.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky may have been trying to build personal rapport by sharing his location. The intent is understandable but the execution introduced doubt rather than connection.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag this as a legitimacy-erosion pattern. The coaching point: customers don't need to know where the rep sits — they need to know the company can serve them locally. Bucky should practice a clean answer to 'where are you based?' that keeps the focus on the customer's market.

quote: Please just send my husband email, and and we'll go from there.
stewart_read: Debbie's exit surfaces Mike for the first time as the decision-maker — and Bucky's response is to accept the redirect and offer a text. The protocols.spouse_decision section is explicit: the correct move is to include the spouse in the appointment, not to route around them. Bucky had two options here: (1) ask for Mike's availability and attempt to book a time for both of them, or (2) ask Debbie to have Mike call back. Instead, he offered a passive text with no time anchor, no Mike inclusion, and no curiosity hook. The call ends with a soft callback that has no structural reason to convert.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have assessed that pushing for Mike's involvement would have ended the call harder. Rejected — the call was already ending; the only question was whether it ended with a locked next step or a vague 'text me back.'
coaching_implication: Manager should use this as a clean protocols.spouse_decision teaching moment. The coaching question: 'At 02:55, what did you have to lose by asking for Mike's availability?' The answer is nothing — the call was already closing. Bucky should practice the spouse-inclusion pivot as a default close when the decision-maker surfaces at exit.

quote: I can hear. I can just text you, and you can always have my number. And if you want Okay. Text me back, you can.
stewart_read: Bucky's close is a passive text offer with no time anchor, no computer/no-driving ask, no 'is that fair' handshake, and no Mike included. Every required element of the button_up protocol is absent. The offer — 'text me back if you want' — places all forward motion on Debbie, who has already signaled low interest three times. This is not a conditional booking; it is a soft exit with a business card.
alternative_hypothesis: Given Debbie's posture, a full button-up attempt may have felt tone-deaf. Partially valid — but the minimum viable close here was a time anchor ('I'll text you — is tomorrow afternoon a good time for you and Mike to take a look together?') rather than an open-ended 'text me back if you want.'
coaching_implication: Manager should walk through the button_up checklist against this close: zero of six required elements present. The coaching point is not that Bucky should have forced a full appointment — it's that even a soft close needs a time anchor and a decision-maker inclusion to have any conversion probability.
